name: Applications of Integrals — Area Under a Curve
whatIsIt: One of integration's biggest jobs is finding the AREA under a curve. The area between the curve y = f(x), the x-axis, and the lines x = a and x = b is the definite integral ∫ from a to b of f(x) dx. Simple shapes check it: the area under y = 3 from x = 0 to 4 is a 3-by-4 rectangle = 12, and the integral gives the same.
whyNeeded: Areas under curves are totals: distance from a speed graph, work from a force graph, total rainfall from a rate. When the top edge is curved, only integration gives the exact area.
realLifeUses: Total distance = area under a speed–time graph
Work done = area under a force–distance graph
Total rainfall from a rainfall-rate graph
Area of land with a curved boundary

vocabulary.meaning: The region between the curve, the x-axis, and two vertical lines x = a and x = b.
Area = ∫ from a to b of f(x) dx (for a curve above the axis).
The left and right x-values that bound the region.
The function whose derivative is f(x); used to evaluate the integral.

story.title: The Sloping Field
story.text: Ravi's field has a straight slanted edge following y = 2x from x = 0 to x = 3. 'What's the area?' asks Nani. Ravi integrates: area = ∫ from 0 to 3 of 2x dx = [x²] from 0 to 3 = 9 square units. He checks with the triangle rule (½ × base × height = ½ × 3 × 6 = 9) — they match! 'The integral gave the exact area,' Ravi grins.
story.extractedProblem: Find the area under y = 2x from x = 0 to x = 3.
story.answerInStory: 9 — ∫ from 0 to 3 of 2x dx = [x²]₀³ = 9 (and ½ × 3 × 6 = 9 agrees).

visual.caption: The shaded region under y = 2x from x = 0 to 3 is the area ∫₀³ 2x dx = 9 — matching the triangle ½ × 3 × 6 = 9.

teachingGallery.title: Integral = area
teachingGallery.note: Area under y = f(x) from a to b is ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx; check with simple shapes.

examples.caption: Constant y = 3 over width 4: a rectangle of area 12 (∫₀⁴ 3 dx = 12).
Under y = 2x: a triangle, ½ × 3 × 6 = 9, matching the integral.
Area under y = x from 0 to 4 is ∫₀⁴ x dx = 8.

standardMethod.summary: Area under y = f(x) from a to b = ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx. Find the antiderivative, substitute the two limits, and subtract.
steps: Write the area as ∫ from a to b of f(x) dx.
Substitute the upper limit b, then the lower limit a.
Sanity-check with a simple shape (rectangle or triangle) when you can.

workedExamples.problem: Find the area under y = 3 from x = 0 to x = 4.
Find the area under y = 2x from x = 0 to x = 3.

commonMistakes.mistake: Finding the antiderivative but forgetting to substitute and subtract the limits.
commonMistakes.fix: Area is a DEFINITE integral: put in the top and bottom x-values and subtract. Don't stop at the antiderivative.

commonMistakes.mistake: Differentiating instead of integrating to find an area.
commonMistakes.fix: Area under a curve is an INTEGRAL (adding up), not a derivative. Integrate f(x) between the limits.

challenge.q: Area under y = 2x from x = 0 to x = 5 = [x²]₀⁵ = ?

questions.q: Area under y = 5 from x = 0 to x = 3?
Area under y = 2x from x = 0 to x = 3?
Area under y = x from x = 0 to x = 2?
The area under y = f(x) from a to b is…

options.label: ∫ from a to b of f(x) dx

questions.answer: ∫ from a to b of f(x) dx

teachBackPrompt: Show a grown-up that the area under y = 2x from 0 to 3 is the integral [x²]₀³ = 9, and check it as a triangle (½ × 3 × 6 = 9). Explain area under a curve = a definite integral.

revisionCard.summary: Area under y = f(x) from a to b = ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx. Find the antiderivative, substitute the limits, subtract. Check with shapes: y = 3 over width 4 → 12; y = 2x from 0 to 3 → 9 (triangle ½·3·6). Area is integration (adding up), never a derivative.
